summary:
Claim: The inheritance mode is X-linked dominant.
- Affected males don't pass the condition to their daughters but may pass it to sons via carrier mothers.
- All affected females will transmit the condition to their offspring regardless of gender.
Evidence: 
- Males are hemizygous for the X chromosome, so an affected male cannot pass a second copy of the mutated gene to his daughters.
- Females can be either heterozygous or homozygous for the mutation and thus can transmit it to both genders of their offspring.
Answer: C
The conclusion is supported by the consistent pattern of inheritance observed, which fits the expectations of X-linked dominant inheritance. The specific mechanism explains why only affected females consistently transmit the condition, whereas affected males do not directly impact their daughters' status. This aligns with known principles of X-linked inheritance, reinforcing the choice of X-linked dominant as the correct answer. ```plaintext
Claim: The inheritance mode is X-linked dominant.

-Evidence:
• Affected males do not pass the condition to their daughters but could potentially pass it to sons through carrier mothers.
• All affected females will invariably transmit the condition to their offspring, regardless of whether they are male or female.

Answer: C

This claim is substantiated by observing that in X-linked dominant inheritance, males (who carry only one X chromosome) cannot pass on the mutant allele to their daughters, thereby keeping them unaffected. Conversely, females (who carry two X chromosomes), even if they are carriers themselves, will always pass on one X chromosome to each child, meaning there’s a strong likelihood that at least some of their children—both boys and girls—will inherit the condition. This pattern perfectly mirrors the clinical observations mentioned in the question, confirming that the inheritance follows an X-linked dominant model.
```
